**Architecture**: Single-activity Jetpack Compose app (Material3, compose-bom 2024.06.00). `MainActivity.kt` calls `HelldeckAppUI()` in `app/src/main/java/com/helldeck/ui/Scenes.kt`, which switches on a plain `Scene` enum held in `GameNightViewModel` (`ui/vm/GameNightViewModel.kt`, ~1,990 lines — the self-declared "ONLY ViewModel for game flow"). `navigation-compose` is a dependency and `ui/nav/Screen.kt` defines routes, but real navigation is just `vm.scene = Scene.X` plus a 10-deep ArrayDeque back stack.

**1. Session flow**: Onboarding (first run) → HOME (`ui/scenes/HomeScene.kt`): spice slider 1–5, "Start the Chaos" → `vm.startRound(null)` (random game via `pickNextGame()` — category rotation, comeback rule gives last place easy voting games). Specific game selection via `GamePickerSheet` in the "More" sheet → `startRound(gameId)`. Rollcall (`RollcallScene.kt`) is optional attendance toggling; Players scene adds/removes players (Room DB, defaults "Jay/Pip/Mo" seeded if empty). ROUND (`ui/scenes/RoundScene.kt`) drives 5 phases from `ui/state/RoundState.kt`: INTRO ("SEAT N's TURN — Pass the phone!") → INPUT (interaction UI + countdown) → REVEAL (points flash + QuickReactionBar, 5s auto-advance = implicit MEH) → FEEDBACK (`FeedbackScene.kt`, LOL/MEH/TRASH, auto-advance countdown) → DONE → `commitFeedbackAndNext()` records Thompson-sampling reward (`engine.recordOutcome`), increments `turnIdx = (turnIdx+1) % poolSize`, and immediately calls `startRound()` again. The loop never ends on its own.

**Pass-and-play mechanics**: sequential hot-seat voting in `ui/scenes/GameFlowComponents.kt`. `AvatarVoteFlow`/`ABVoteFlow` hold `voterIndex` as composable-local `remember` state; each player takes the phone, taps a tile (visibly "LOCKED" on screen), hits "Lock & Next", the selection resets, and the phone passes. Secrecy is purely social — subtitle: "Pass the phone to each player. The room only sees the final damage." Roast Consensus: card shown, N sequential secret votes on a seat grid, then `resolveRoastConsensus()` in the VM awards +2 to each majority voter, +1 heat bonus at 80% agreement.

**2. State machine & coupling**: All game logic lives inside `GameNightViewModel`: `votesAvatar`/`votesAB` in-memory maps, ~14 per-game mutable state vars (e.g. `hotSeatImposterId`, `overUnderLine`), `resolveInteraction()` dispatching to 12 private `resolve*()` scoring methods. `engine/GameMetadata.kt` declares the 14 games (timer, interaction type, 3–16 players). Coupling to single-device is total: voting iteration lives in composables, phase advancement mutates `vm.scene` mid-resolution, timers are local `LaunchedEffect { delay(1000) }` loops, points persist to local Room (`repo.db.players()`). Notably, `ui/interactions/` (16 renderer files incl. `InteractionRenderer.kt`) and `engine/SeatManager.kt` are dead parallel systems — referenced nowhere outside themselves.

**3. Timers, scoring, length**: Per-game `timerSec` 10–60s (Roast 20, Confession 15, Poison Pitch 30, Fill-In 60, Red Flag 45, Taboo 60, Scatterblast 10, Over/Under 20). `engine/Config.kt` defaults: win=3, room_heat_bonus=2, threshold 0.65 (0.70 spicy), trash_penalty=-1 — but most resolvers hardcode +2/+1/-1 "per HDRealRules.md" instead. **There is no win condition**: no target score, no round cap. Every 20 rounds a "ROUNDS SURVIVED" dialog suggests stopping; the game ends only when someone finds "End Game" buried under "More ▾" in FeedbackScene, opening `EndGameVotingDialog.kt` — scoreboard sorted by `sessionPoints` (position 1 = winner) plus MVP/Dud card voting. A round is ~1–3 min (intro + timer + N sequential phone passes + 5s reveal + feedback), so a "game" is however long the group tolerates.

**4. Per-player remote screens**: UI is fully Compose; the interaction flows are self-contained composables (players + callbacks) so client rendering is portable. The blocker is state ownership: authority is a device-local ViewModel with composable-local vote iteration, synchronous resolvers reading in-memory maps, and scene navigation entangled with scoring (`resolveInteraction()` sets `roundState.phase` AND `vm.scene`). A Jackbox conversion needs the resolvers + `RoundState` extracted into a headless host-authoritative state machine with per-client input channels — the `resolve*()` math is portable pure Kotlin; everything around it must be rewritten.

**5. Networking**: Zero. `AndroidManifest.xml` has no INTERNET permission (only VIBRATE, FOREGROUND_SERVICE, BIND_DEVICE_ADMIN). No okhttp/retrofit/ktor/Nearby/Bluetooth in `app/build.gradle`. `llm/proxy/ProxyLLM.kt` is a deterministic test stub, not a network proxy; `ErrorBoundary.kt` merely classifies `SocketException` for messages. There is no sync, lobby, or multi-device code of any kind — the browser-join model is a greenfield build on the backend side.","key_facts":["Game loop lives in app/src/main/java/com/helldeck/ui/vm/GameNightViewModel.kt (~1,990 lines) — navigation, voting maps, per-game state, and all 12 resolve*() scoring methods in one class; Every 20 rounds a "ROUNDS SURVIVED" dialog suggests stopping; the game ends only when someone finds "End Game" buried under "More ▾" in FeedbackScene, opening `EndGameVotingDialog.kt` — scoreboard sorted by `sessionPoints` (position 1 = winner) plus MVP/Dud card voting. A round is ~1–3 min (intro + timer + N sequential phone passes + 5s reveal + feedback), so a "game" is however long the group tolerates.

**4. Per-player remote screens**: UI is fully Compose; the interaction flows are self-contained composables (players + callbacks) so client rendering is portable. The blocker is state ownership: authority is a device-local ViewModel with composable-local vote iteration, synchronous resolvers reading in-memory maps, and scene navigation entangled with scoring (`resolveInteraction()` sets `roundState.phase` AND `vm.scene`). A Jackbox conversion needs the resolvers + `RoundState` extracted into a headless host-authoritative state machine with per-client input channels — the `resolve*()` math is portable pure Kotlin; everything around it must be rewritten.

**5. Networking**: Zero. `AndroidManifest.xml` has no INTERNET permission (only VIBRATE, FOREGROUND_SERVICE, BIND_DEVICE_ADMIN). No okhttp/retrofit/ktor/Nearby/Bluetooth in `app/build.gradle`. `llm/proxy/ProxyLLM.kt` is a deterministic test stub, not a network proxy; `ErrorBoundary.kt` merely classifies `SocketException` for messages. There is no sync, lobby, or multi-device code of any kind — the browser-join model is a greenfield build on the backend side.","key_facts":["Game loop lives in app/src/main/java/com/helldeck/ui/vm/GameNightViewModel.kt (~1,990 lines) — navigation, voting maps, per-game state, and all 12 resolve*() scoring methods in one class; phases INTRO->INPUT->REVEAL->FEEDBACK->DONE defined in ui/state/RoundState.kt.","Navigation is a plain Scene enum switch in ui/Scenes.kt (HelldeckAppUI) with vm.scene mutation, not the navigation-compose routes in ui/nav/Screen.kt.","Pass-and-play voting (ui/scenes/GameFlowComponents.kt AvatarVoteFlow/ABVoteFlow) is sequential hot-seat: voterIndex is composable-local remember state; each player taps a visible choice, hits Lock & Next, passes the phone; secrecy is purely social trust.","Round loop is infinite: commitFeedbackAndNext() advances turnIdx and immediately calls startRound(); no win condition or target score exists — ending requires the End Game button hidden under 'More' in FeedbackScene, every 20 rounds a prompt suggests stopping, winner = highest sessionPoints in EndGameVotingDialog.kt.","The 14 games are declared in engine/GameMetadata.kt with per-game timerSec 10-60s and interaction enums; engine/Config.kt defaults (win=3, heat bonus=2, threshold 0.65) are mostly bypassed by hardcoded +2/+1/-1 values in resolvers citing HDRealRules.md.","UI is 100% Jetpack Compose Material3, single MainActivity; interaction flows are portable composables but state authority is device-local (Room DB for points, LaunchedEffect delay loops for timers, resolveInteraction() mutates both roundState.phase and vm.scene).","Zero networking exists: no INTERNET permission in AndroidManifest.xml, no HTTP/socket/Nearby/Bluetooth dependencies in app/build.gradle; ProxyLLM.kt is a test heuristic stub — a Jackbox-style browser-join model is a full greenfield backend build.","Two dead parallel systems exist and could confuse a redesign: ui/interactions/ (16 renderer files incl. content.content:
1	# read:content-pipeline
2	
3	# HELLDECK Card Pipeline Map
4	
5	## 1. Runtime chain
6	Entry: `GameNightViewModel` (app/src/main/java/com/helldeck/ui/vm/GameNightViewModel.kt:193) gets the singleton `GameEngine` from `ContentEngineProvider.get()` (content/engine/ContentEngineProvider.kt). `GameEngine.next()` (content/engine/GameEngine.kt:49) tries, in order:
7	1. **LLMCardGeneratorV2** (content/generator/LLMCardGeneratorV2.kt) — "quality-first" LLM path. Internally: if `llm?.isReady == true`, 3 attempts × 6s timeout; else `fallbackToGold()` (random gold card via `GoldCardsLoader.getRandomFallback`) then `fallbackToTemplates()` (CardGeneratorV3).
8	2. **CardGeneratorV3** (content/generator/CardGeneratorV3.kt) — template/madlib path; itself serves gold ~40% of the time up front (`rng.nextDouble() < 0.40`, line 56) and ends in `goldFallback()`.
9	3. **Legacy V2 templates** via `ContextualSelector` + `TemplateEngine` + `Augmentor` paraphrase (assets/templates_v2 has only roast_consensus.json).
10	4. `createGoldFallback()` — hardcoded per-interaction-type stock cards.
11	
12	**What actually fires: gold cards, almost always.** Two compounding reasons: (a) `ContentEngineProvider.initializeLocalLLM()` returns null on cold start (model .gguf is copied/initialized asynchronously via `scheduleModelPreparation`), and `LLMCardGeneratorV2` captures that null `llm` in its constructor — the async-loaded LLM is never injected into the already-built generator, so the LLM path is effectively dead for the whole process lifetime. (b) Even when LLM is skipped, `generate()` returns `llmResult ?: fallbackToGold(...) ?: fallbackToTemplates(...)` — gold covers all 14 games (917 cards), so PRIORITY 1 returns a **uniformly random, spice-filtered gold card** (quality_score and feedback ignored in `getRandomFallback`), it passes `GameContractValidator`, and the engine returns. The V3 madlib engine, Thompson sampling, and augmentor are all bypassed in normal play. Config defaults (engine/Config.kt:144-145): `safe_mode_gold_only=false`, `enable_v3_generator=true`.
13	
14	## 2. Templates_v3 + lexicons_v2 (madlib slot-filling)
15	Yes — `TemplateBlueprint` (assets/templates_v3/*.json, loaded by `BlueprintRepositoryV3` in content/generator/RepositoriesV3.kt) is a segment list of `{type:text}` and `{type:slot, slot_type:X}`. `CardGeneratorV3.tryGenerate()` fills each slot from `LexiconRepositoryV2` (assets/lexicons_v2/<slot_type>.json; entries carry text/tags/tone/spice/locality/pluralizable/needs_article), with tone-biased selection, mods (upper/title/plural), and article insertion. Real assembled examples (from frozen audit CSVs):
16	- ROAST_CONSENSUS bp1 ("Most likely to {sketchy_action} because {social_reason}."): *"Most likely to leave the groupchat dramatically because their main love language is screenshots."*
17	- POISON_PITCH bp2 ({selfish_behaviors}/{would_you_rather_costs}): *"Would you rather live with never hit traffic or survive a pet that sheds emotional baggage and fur?"* (note grammar breakage — slot text doesn't fit the frame).
18	- RED_FLAG_RALLY bp1 ({perks_plus} but {red_flag_issue}): *"Smash or pass: unlimited airline lounge access for life but they refer to themselves in third person."*
19	Gates on this path: session dedupe (200-card window), `SemanticValidator` (assets/model/semantic_compatibility.json, threshold 0.50), coherence gate (6–20 words, repetition ≤0.25, banned tokens/phrases from assets/model/banned.json, logit.json logistic model, pairings.json pair scores), `HumorScorer` ≥0.60, `ComedyScienceValidator`. Thresholds in assets/model/rules.yaml; attempt budgets per game (4–5).
20	
21	## 3. On-device LLM
22	Prompts: `ComedySciencePrompts` (content/generator/ComedySciencePrompts.kt) — Benign Violation Theory system prompt + per-game user prompt with 10 gold examples (weighted rotation by quality_score, `GoldCardsLoader.getExamplesForGame`) + spice guidance. GenConfig: 150 tokens, temp 0.5–0.9 by spice. Output must be bare JSON; `parseAndValidateResponse` then `validateQuality` (heuristic score ≥0.65 + `ComedyScienceValidator.validate` — banned clichés instant-fail, specificity 0–5, visual imagery 0–3, game-mechanic alignment, per-game minimums). Contract check via `GameContractValidator` (content/validation/). Models shipped in APK assets/models/: qwen2.5-1.5b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf (1.12 GB) and tinyllama-1.1b-chat-v1.0.Q4_K_M.gguf (669 MB) — ~1.8 GB of assets. Native bridge `libhelldeck_llama` built from app/CMakeLists.txt + app/src/main/cpp/llama_jni.cpp against third_party/llama.cpp (stub .so if submodule absent). Rejection rates unmeasurable in production because the path never runs (see §1); `LlamaCppLLM.generate` returns the *user prompt* on failure.
23	
24	## 4. Thompson sampling / LOL-MEH-TRASH
25	`ContextualSelector` (content/engine/ContextualSelector.kt) — per-template Beta(α,β) posteriors, Marsaglia-Tsang gamma sampling, diversity penalties, tag affinity. Feedback: `GameNightViewModel.commitFeedbackAndNext()` (line 1560) → `Rewards.fromCounts` (content/quality/Rewards.kt: LOL=1.0, MEH=0.35, TRASH=0.0) → `GameEngine.recordOutcome()` → `selector.update()` (gain = Config.learning.alpha = 0.4) + persisted `TemplateStatEntity`; priors re-seeded at boot. **It only adjusts legacy-V2 template selection, which is bypassed** — V3 blueprint ordering uses static assets/model/priors.json; gold draws are uniform random; and gold/LLM card IDs embed timestamps so recorded stats never re-key to anything reusable. A second feedback store (`CardFeedbackDao`/`CardScoreEntity`, data/CardFeedbackEntities.kt) computes per-card scores with AUTO_EXCLUDE 0.15 / LOW_PRIORITY 0.35 thresholds in GoldCardsLoader — but no caller ever passes `cardScores`, so it's plumbed and disconnected. Net: feedback adjusts nothing the player sees.
26	
27	## 5. Quality gates / audits
28	Tools: tools/ (card_quality_verifier.py, calibrate_quality.py, card_audit_diff.py, quality_pipeline.sh, quality_summarize.py, lexicon_lint.py, dupcheck.py); gradle tasks `:app:cardAudit` / `:app:cardQuality` (app/build.gradle:292,315); offline gold/lexicon rewrite scripts in scripts/ (generate_gold_cards.py, rewrite_gold_*.py, rewrite_lex_g*.py). Baselines: docs/card_audit_baselines/ (2025-11-03, POISON_PITCH + ROAST_CONSENSUS only). Conclusions: docs/quality_summary.md reports 100% pass rates but mediocre avg scores (~0.65–0.68) with LOW_HUMOR×59 (RED_FLAG_RALLY) and NOT_TARGETED×13 (ROAST_CONSENSUS); docs/quality_ai_summary.md AI-judge humor01 ranges 0.475 (FILL_IN_FINISHER, which also scores 0.5 makesSense) to 0.85; both docs still include removed legacy games — stale. Baseline CSVs show heavy repetition within one seed (same slot fill reused across consecutive cards).
29	
30	## KEY FACTS
31	- In production the card pipeline serves an almost-uniformly-random curated gold card (917 in assets/gold/gold_cards_v2.json) nearly 100% of the time; LLM, template/madlib, and Thompson-sampling paths are effectively bypassed.
32	- The on-device LLM is dead code at runtime: ContentEngineProvider.buildGameEngine constructs LLMCardGeneratorV2 with the then-null localLLM (model .gguf copies/initializes asynchronously), and the constructor val is never updated, so llm.isReady is never true for card generation.
33	- LLMCardGeneratorV2.generate() falls back gold-first (GoldCardsLoader.getRandomFallback ignores quality_score and feedback — pure random by game+spice), so even CardGeneratorV3's madlib engine rarely runs; CardGeneratorV3 itself also serves gold 40% of the time.
34	- GameEngine.next (app/src/main/java/com/helldeck/content/engine/GameEngine.kt) priority chain: LLMCardGeneratorV2 → CardGeneratorV3 (templates_v3+lexicons_v2) → legacy V2 templates via ContextualSelector → hardcoded createGoldFallback; every path passes GameContractValidator.
35	- templates_v3 are madlib blueprints (text+slot segments); slots fill from lexicons_v2/<slot_type>.json entries (text/tone/spice/locality); example output: 'Most likely to leave the groupchat dramatically because their main love language is screenshots.' Grammar breakage occurs when slot text doesn't fit the frame ('live with never hit traffic').
36	- LOL/MEH/TRASH feedback (Rewards.kt: 1.0/0.35/0.0) updates ContextualSelector Beta posteriors and Room TemplateStatEntity, but that selector only drives the bypassed legacy-V2 path; gold/LLM card IDs embed timestamps so learning never re-keys; the CardFeedback/CardScoreEntity system with exclude thresholds is never wired into any card selection call.
37	- Two GGUF models ship in the APK assets/models/: qwen2.5-1.5b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf (1.12 GB) and tinyllama-1.1b-chat-v1.0.Q4_K_M.gguf (669 MB) — ~1.8 GB payload for a feature that never fires; native bridge libhelldeck_llama builds from app/CMakeLists.txt + third_party/llama.cpp submodule.
38	- LLM prompt system (ComedySciencePrompts.kt) uses Benign Violation Theory + 10 weighted-rotated gold examples per game; outputs validated by ComedyScienceValidator (banned clichés, specificity 0-5, visual imagery 0-3, mechanic alignment) and a heuristic quality score ≥0.65.
39	- V3 generation gates live in assets/model/ (rules.yaml: humor_threshold 0.60, semantic_threshold 0.50, 6-20 words; banned.json, pairings.json, priors.json, logit.json) with per-game attempt budgets of 4-5.
40	- Past audits (docs/quality_summary.md, docs/quality_ai_summary.md, docs/card_audit_baselines/ frozen 2025-11-03) report 100% gate pass rates but mediocre quality: avg scores ~0.67, LOW_HUMOR×59 in RED_FLAG_RALLY, NOT_TARGETED×13 in ROAST_CONSENSUS, AI-judge humor as low as 0.475 (FILL_IN_FINISHER also 0.5 makesSense); docs still reference removed legacy games and only 2 of 14 games have baselines.
41	- Quality tooling: gradle tasks :app:cardAudit and :app:cardQuality (app/build.gradle:292/315), tools/quality_pipeline.sh auto-calibrates per-game minHumor thresholds toward a target pass rate, scripts/rewrite_gold_*.py and rewrite_lex_g*.py are offline content rewrite passes.
42	- Config defaults (engine/Config.kt:144-145): safe_mode_gold_only=false, enable_v3_generator=true; asset validation failure at boot forces gold-only mode; learning gain Config.learning.alpha=0.4.

2. **CardGeneratorV3** (content/generator/CardGeneratorV3.kt) — template/madlib path; itself serves gold ~40% of the time up front (`rng.nextDouble() < 0.40`, line 56) and ends in `goldFallback()`.
3. **Legacy V2 templates** via `ContextualSelector` + `TemplateEngine` + `Augmentor` paraphrase (assets/templates_v2 has only roast_consensus.json).
4. `createGoldFallback()` — hardcoded per-interaction-type stock cards.

**What actually fires: gold cards, almost always.** Two compounding reasons: (a) `ContentEngineProvider.initializeLocalLLM()` returns null on cold start (model .gguf is copied/initialized asynchronously via `scheduleModelPreparation`), and `LLMCardGeneratorV2` captures that null `llm` in its constructor — the async-loaded LLM is never injected into the already-built generator, so the LLM path is effectively dead for the whole process lifetime. (b) Even when LLM is skipped, `generate()` returns `llmResult ?: fallbackToGold(...) ?: fallbackToTemplates(...)` — gold covers all 14 games (917 cards), so PRIORITY 1 returns a **uniformly random, spice-filtered gold card** (quality_score and feedback ignored in `getRandomFallback`), it passes `GameContractValidator`, and the engine returns. The V3 madlib engine, Thompson sampling, and augmentor are all bypassed in normal play. Config defaults (engine/Config.kt:144-145): `safe_mode_gold_only=false`, `enable_v3_generator=true`.

## 2. Templates_v3 + lexicons_v2 (madlib slot-filling)
Yes — `TemplateBlueprint` (assets/templates_v3/*.json, loaded by `BlueprintRepositoryV3` in content/generator/RepositoriesV3.kt) is a segment list of `{type:text}` and `{type:slot, slot_type:X}`. `CardGeneratorV3.tryGenerate()` fills each slot from `LexiconRepositoryV2` (assets/lexicons_v2/<slot_type>.json; entries carry text/tags/tone/spice/locality/pluralizable/needs_article), with tone-biased selection, mods (upper/title/plural), and article insertion. Real assembled examples (from frozen audit CSVs):
- ROAST_CONSENSUS bp1 ("Most likely to {sketchy_action} because {social_reason}."): *"Most likely to leave the groupchat dramatically because their main love language is screenshots."*
- POISON_PITCH bp2 ({selfish_behaviors}/{would_you_rather_costs}): *"Would you rather live with never hit traffic or survive a pet that sheds emotional baggage and fur?"* (note grammar breakage — slot text doesn't fit the frame).
- RED_FLAG_RALLY bp1 ({perks_plus} but {red_flag_issue}): *"Smash or pass: unlimited airline lounge access for life but they refer to themselves in third person."*
Gates on this path: session dedupe (200-card window), `SemanticValidator` (assets/model/semantic_compatibility.json, threshold 0.50), coherence gate (6–20 words, repetition ≤0.25, banned tokens/phrases from assets/model/banned.json, logit.json logistic model, pairings.json pair scores), `HumorScorer` ≥0.60, `ComedyScienceValidator`. Thresholds in assets/model/rules.yaml; attempt budgets per game (4–5).

## 3. On-device LLM
Prompts: `ComedySciencePrompts` (content/generator/ComedySciencePrompts.kt) — Benign Violation Theory system prompt + per-game user prompt with 10 gold examples (weighted rotation by quality_score, `GoldCardsLoader.getExamplesForGame`) + spice guidance. GenConfig: 150 tokens, temp 0.5–0.9 by spice. Output must be bare JSON; `parseAndValidateResponse` then `validateQuality` (heuristic score ≥0.65 + `ComedyScienceValidator.validate` — banned clichés instant-fail, specificity 0–5, visual imagery 0–3, game-mechanic alignment, per-game minimums). Contract check via `GameContractValidator` (content/validation/). Models shipped in APK assets/models/: qwen2.5-1.5b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf (1.12 GB) and tinyllama-1.1b-chat-v1.0.Q4_K_M.gguf (669 MB) — ~1.8 GB of assets. Native bridge `libhelldeck_llama` built from app/CMakeLists.txt + app/src/main/cpp/llama_jni.cpp against third_party/llama.cpp (stub .so if submodule absent). Rejection rates unmeasurable in production because the path never runs (see §1); `LlamaCppLLM.generate` returns the *user prompt* on failure.

## 4. Thompson sampling / LOL-MEH-TRASH
`ContextualSelector` (content/engine/ContextualSelector.kt) — per-template Beta(α,β) posteriors, Marsaglia-Tsang gamma sampling, diversity penalties, tag affinity. Feedback: `GameNightViewModel.commitFeedbackAndNext()` (line 1560) → `Rewards.fromCounts` (content/quality/Rewards.kt: LOL=1.0, MEH=0.35, TRASH=0.0) → `GameEngine.recordOutcome()` → `selector.update()` (gain = Config.learning.alpha = 0.4) + persisted `TemplateStatEntity`; priors re-seeded at boot. **It only adjusts legacy-V2 template selection, which is bypassed** — V3 blueprint ordering uses static assets/model/priors.json; gold draws are uniform random; and gold/LLM card IDs embed timestamps so recorded stats never re-key to anything reusable. A second feedback store (`CardFeedbackDao`/`CardScoreEntity`, data/CardFeedbackEntities.kt) computes per-card scores with AUTO_EXCLUDE 0.15 / LOW_PRIORITY 0.35 thresholds in GoldCardsLoader — but no caller ever passes `cardScores`, so it's plumbed and disconnected. Net: feedback adjusts nothing the player sees.

## 5. Quality gates / audits
Tools: tools/ (card_quality_verifier.py, calibrate_quality.py, card_audit_diff.py, quality_pipeline.sh, quality_summarize.py, lexicon_lint.py, dupcheck.py); gradle tasks `:app:cardAudit` / `:app:cardQuality` (app/build.gradle:292,315); offline gold/lexicon rewrite scripts in scripts/ (generate_gold_cards.py, rewrite_gold_*.py, rewrite_lex_g*.py). Baselines: docs/card_audit_baselines/ (2025-11-03, POISON_PITCH + ROAST_CONSENSUS only). Conclusions: docs/quality_summary.md reports 100% pass rates but mediocre avg scores (~0.65–0.68) with LOW_HUMOR×59 (RED_FLAG_RALLY) and NOT_TARGETED×13 (ROAST_CONSENSUS); docs/quality_ai_summary.md AI-judge humor01 ranges 0.475 (FILL_IN_FINISHER, which also scores 0.5 makesSense) to 0.85; both docs still include removed legacy games — stale. Baseline CSVs show heavy repetition within one seed (same slot fill reused across consecutive cards).

## KEY FACTS
- In production the card pipeline serves an almost-uniformly-random curated gold card (917 in assets/gold/gold_cards_v2.json) nearly 100% of the time; LLM, template/madlib, and Thompson-sampling paths are effectively bypassed.
- The on-device LLM is dead code at runtime: ContentEngineProvider.buildGameEngine constructs LLMCardGeneratorV2 with the then-null localLLM (model .gguf copies/initializes asynchronously), and the constructor val is never updated, so llm.isReady is never true for card generation.
- LLMCardGeneratorV2.generate() falls back gold-first (GoldCardsLoader.getRandomFallback ignores quality_score and feedback — pure random by game+spice), so even CardGeneratorV3's madlib engine rarely runs; CardGeneratorV3 itself also serves gold 40% of the time.
- GameEngine.next (app/src/main/java/com/helldeck/content/engine/GameEngine.kt) priority chain: LLMCardGeneratorV2 → CardGeneratorV3 (templates_v3+lexicons_v2) → legacy V2 templates via ContextualSelector → hardcoded createGoldFallback; every path passes GameContractValidator.
- templates_v3 are madlib blueprints (text+slot segments); slots fill from lexicons_v2/<slot_type>.json entries (text/tone/spice/locality); example output: 'Most likely to leave the groupchat dramatically because their main love language is screenshots.' Grammar breakage occurs when slot text doesn't fit the frame ('live with never hit traffic').
- LOL/MEH/TRASH feedback (Rewards.kt: 1.0/0.35/0.0) updates ContextualSelector Beta posteriors and Room TemplateStatEntity, but that selector only drives the bypassed legacy-V2 path; gold/LLM card IDs embed timestamps so learning never re-keys; the CardFeedback/CardScoreEntity system with exclude thresholds is never wired into any card selection call.
- Two GGUF models ship in the APK assets/models/: qwen2.5-1.5b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf (1.12 GB) and tinyllama-1.1b-chat-v1.0.Q4_K_M.gguf (669 MB) — ~1.8 GB payload for a feature that never fires; native bridge libhelldeck_llama builds from app/CMakeLists.txt + third_party/llama.cpp submodule.
- LLM prompt system (ComedySciencePrompts.kt) uses Benign Violation Theory + 10 weighted-rotated gold examples per game; outputs validated by ComedyScienceValidator (banned clichés, specificity 0-5, visual imagery 0-3, mechanic alignment) and a heuristic quality score ≥0.65.
- V3 generation gates live in assets/model/ (rules.yaml: humor_threshold 0.60, semantic_threshold 0.50, 6-20 words; banned.json, pairings.json, priors.json, logit.json) with per-game attempt budgets of 4-5.
- Past audits (docs/quality_summary.md, docs/quality_ai_summary.md, docs/card_audit_baselines/ frozen 2025-11-03) report 100% gate pass rates but mediocre quality: avg scores ~0.67, LOW_HUMOR×59 in RED_FLAG_RALLY, NOT_TARGETED×13 in ROAST_CONSENSUS, AI-judge humor as low as 0.475 (FILL_IN_FINISHER also 0.5 makesSense); docs still reference removed legacy games and only 2 of 14 games have baselines.
- Quality tooling: gradle tasks :app:cardAudit and :app:cardQuality (app/build.gradle:292/315), tools/quality_pipeline.sh auto-calibrates per-game minHumor thresholds toward a target pass rate, scripts/rewrite_gold_*.py and rewrite_lex_g*.py are offline content rewrite passes.
- Config defaults (engine/Config.kt:144-145): safe_mode_gold_only=false, enable_v3_generator=true; asset validation failure at boot forces gold-only mode; learning gain Config.learning.alpha=0.4.

1	# read:rules-interactions
2	
3	## Per-game analysis (N = player count; handoffs = forced device passes/round today)
4	
5	**1. Roast Consensus** — Loop: prompt, everyone secretly votes a victim, simultaneous reveal, roast. ~45s. No roles, all vote. SECRET: each vote until reveal. SHARED: prompt, timer, tally. Handoffs: N (every secret vote needs the phone). Multi-device: MUCH BETTER — simultaneous secret vote is the native own-phone mechanic; today handoffs destroy the "no bandwagoning" rule. Risk: card quality is everything; mechanic itself is near-zero cognitive load.
6	
7	**2. Confession or Cap** — Loop: confessor claims TRUE/FALSE aloud, jury votes believe/cap, truth revealed. ~60-90s. Roles: 1 confessor + jury. SECRET: confessor's real answer (must be locked into app privately), jury votes until reveal. SHARED: prompt, claim, verdict. Handoffs: ~N (covert confessor entry + serial jury votes). Multi-device: MUCH BETTER — tamper-proof private truth-lock + simultaneous jury vote; the poker-face performance stays verbal. Risk: needs lie-worthy prompts; weak cards make the answer obvious.
8	
9	**3. Poison Pitch** — Loop: two assigned pitchers each get 30s to verbally defend a terrible option; group votes best pitch. ~2min. Roles: 2 pitchers + audience. SECRET: audience votes only. SHARED: dilemma, assignments, timer. Handoffs: ~N-2 for votes. Multi-device: BETTER (modestly) — private per-pitcher assignment + clean simultaneous vote; the comedy is spoken and unaffected. Risk: double spotlight; dies with two shy pitchers; card balance (both options equally bad) is critical.
10	
11	**4. Fill-In Finisher** — Loop: Judge speaks setup, everyone else writes a punchline in 60s, Judge reads anonymously and picks. ~2-3min. Roles: rotating Judge + writers. SECRET: every submission + authorship. SHARED: prompt, setup, read-aloud, winner. Handoffs: N-1 serial typing passes — the single worst flow in the app; serial typing kills anonymity AND pacing. Multi-device: MUCH BETTER — this is literally Quiplash; simultaneous anonymous writing is impossible on one phone. Risk: writing burden for non-writers; mitigate with suggested-answer button.
12	
13	**5. Red Flag Rally** — Loop: assigned Defender pitches an undateable person for 45s; group votes SMASH/PASS. ~90s. Roles: 1 defender + voters. SECRET: votes. SHARED: perk+red flag card, timer, verdict. Handoffs: ~N-1 votes. Multi-device: BETTER — private binary vote with dramatic synced reveal; pitch stays verbal. Risk: single-spotlight; verdict roast is the payoff, keep it in the room.
14	
15	**6. Hot Seat Imposter** — Loop: Imposter answers 3-5 personal questions as the silent Target; group votes REAL/FAKE. ~3min. Roles: Target (mute), Imposter, questioners/voters. SECRET: role assignment delivery, votes. SHARED: questions, performance, verdict. Handoffs: 1 covert + N-2 votes. Multi-device: BETTER — private role/prompt delivery (the Spyfall pattern) plus question prompts pushed to askers' phones; performance is live. Risk: highest social ceiling AND floor — needs groups who know each other; awkward with strangers.
16	
17	**7. Text Thread Trap** — Loop: player improvises a spoken text reply in an assigned tone (22 tones); group votes survived/failed. ~60s. Roles: 1 performer + judges. SECRET: almost nothing — group must know the tone to judge it. SHARED: incoming text, tone, verdict. Handoffs: ~1 + votes. Multi-device: SAME(+) — pure acting game; phones only distribute the prompt and collect the vote. Risk: heavy acting/spotlight burden; drama kids thrive, shy players freeze; tone list is the strongest content asset in the deck.
18	
19	**8. Taboo Timer** — Loop: Clue-Giver describes target word avoiding 5 forbidden words for 60s while everyone shouts guesses. 60s. Roles: Clue-Giver, Buzzer, guessers. SECRET: target+forbidden FROM guessers; forbidden list TO Buzzer (broken today — Buzzer can't see the list without seeing the word over a shoulder). SHARED: timer, score. Handoffs: 1. Multi-device: BETTER — role-gated screens fix the Buzzer role outright; guessers' phones show only timer. Risk: proven mechanic, lowest risk in the deck; real-time shouting untouched.
20	
21	**9. The Unifying Theory** — Loop: player has 30s to argue why 3 unrelated items are the same; group votes. ~60s. Roles: 1 theorist + jury. SECRET: votes only. SHARED: trio, theory, verdict. Handoffs: 0-1 + votes. Multi-device: SAME — pure verbal improv; phones just vote. Risk: highest cognitive load per round; dead air when the theorist blanks; spice 4+ is where it lands.
22	
23	**10. Title Fight** — Loop: drawer ambush-picks an opponent, instant brain/body/soul duel, first to crack loses. ~15-45s. Roles: 2 fighters + referees. SECRET: challenge until "FIGHT!" SHARED: everything else. Handoffs: 1. Multi-device: SAME/WORSE — the game is physical, in-room, unmediated; phones add only ambush delivery to a random player. Risk: great energy spike; referee disputes are part of the fun.
24	
25	**11. Alibi Drop** — Loop: accused improvises a 30s alibi secretly weaving in 3 mandatory words; jury judges believability then hunts the words. ~90s. Roles: 1 accused + jury. SECRET: the 3 mandatory words (jury's whole job is guessing them). SHARED: accusation, story, verdict. Handoffs: 1 covert read (awkward today). Multi-device: MUCH BETTER — words delivered only to accused; jury submits word-guesses privately for a structured reveal; listeners get an active job. Risk: improv burden on one player, but word-hunting keeps the room engaged.
26	
27	**12. Reality Check** — Loop: Subject secretly self-rates 1-10; group debates ALOUD in front of them and sets one number; simultaneous reveal. ~90s. Roles: 1 subject + group. SECRET: Subject's ego score only. SHARED: the debate — deliberately public; that roast IS the game. Handoffs: 1. Multi-device: BETTER (narrowly) — private ego entry + synced reveal; do NOT privatize the group score or the game dies. Risk: real feelings; the loud debate must stay verbal.
28	
29	**13. Scatterblast** — Loop: hidden-fuse category bomb passes turn (not phone) around the circle; whoever holds the turn at boom loses. 20-60s. Roles: Host taps Next, all shout. SECRET: fuse length (held by app). SHARED: category+letter. Handoffs: 0 — explicitly designed around one stationary device. Multi-device: WORSE — multi-phone timer/audio sync adds latency risk to a game that already has zero handoffs; keep one device as the bomb. Risk: proven Catch-Phrase energy; lowest content risk.
30	
31	**14. Over/Under** — Loop: group sets a betting line aloud, all bet OVER/UNDER, Subject reveals the true number (usually from their own phone). ~90s. Roles: 1 subject + bettors. SECRET: bets (prevent anchoring), true number until reveal. SHARED: stat, line debate (verbal — that's the roast), reveal. Handoffs: ~N-2. Multi-device: MUCH BETTER — private simultaneous betting is native, and the receipt already lives on the subject's phone; subject types the number for a synced reveal. Risk: spice-gate the personal-history stats.
32	
33	## Ranking (best → worst fit for phones-as-controllers)
34	1. Fill-In Finisher 2. Roast Consensus 3. Over/Under 4. Confession or Cap 5. Alibi Drop 6. Hot Seat Imposter 7. Taboo Timer 8. Red Flag Rally 9. Poison Pitch 10. Reality Check 11. Text Thread Trap 12. Unifying Theory 13. Title Fight 14. Scatterblast
35	
36	## Structural takeaway
37	The deck decomposes into three archetypes: (A) simultaneous-private-input games (#1-7 above) that one shared phone actively breaks and own-phones fix; (B) verbal-performance games (#8-12) where phones should only gate roles and collect votes; (C) shared-space physical games (#13-14 in rank) that should keep a single "stage" device. 10 of 14 games end in a group vote — one reusable private-vote + synced-reveal component covers most of the redesign. Cardinal rule: screens carry secrets and votes; mouths carry the comedy. Never move the performance onto the screen.
38	
39	## KEY FACTS
40	- HDRealRules.md (2162 lines) contains full rules for all 14 games plus an AI Card Generation Master Guide with per-game formulas, psychology, and good/bad card examples; FEATURES.md confirms timers, interaction types, and the 3-tier generation stack (LLM -> gold -> template).
41	- Fill-In Finisher is the worst offender on a shared phone (N-1 serial typing handoffs that destroy anonymity and pacing) and the single best multi-device conversion — it is structurally identical to Jackbox's Quiplash.
42	- Simultaneous secret voting/betting (Roast Consensus, Over/Under, Confession or Cap) is the core mechanic one shared phone breaks: today it forces roughly N handoffs per round and enables bandwagoning the rules explicitly forbid.
43	- Three games carry per-player secret payloads that only own-phones deliver cleanly: Alibi Drop (3 mandatory words to the accused only), Hot Seat Imposter (secret role assignment), Taboo Timer (word+forbidden list to clue-giver and buzzer, hidden from guessers — the Buzzer role is broken on one device).
44	- Five games are primarily live verbal performance (Poison Pitch, Red Flag Rally, Text Thread Trap, Unifying Theory, Reality Check) — phones should only gate prompts and collect private votes; the comedy must stay spoken.
45	- Two games get NO benefit or get worse with phones-as-controllers: Scatterblast is explicitly designed around one stationary device with zero handoffs (multi-device timer/audio sync is a liability), and Title Fight is a physical in-room duel.
46	- Ranking best-to-worst multi-device fit: Fill-In Finisher, Roast Consensus, Over/Under, Confession or Cap, Alibi Drop, Hot Seat Imposter, Taboo Timer, Red Flag Rally, Poison Pitch, Reality Check, Text Thread Trap, Unifying Theory, Title Fight, Scatterblast.
47	- 10 of 14 games end in a group vote, so a single reusable private-vote + synchronized-reveal component covers most of the redesign surface.
48	- Reality Check's group score must stay a loud public debate in front of the subject (privatizing it kills the game); only the subject's self-rating is secret.
49	- Over/Under is uniquely phone-native: the 'receipt' (screen time, unread emails, camera roll count) already lives on the subject's own phone.
50	- Rounds are short (15-60s timers per FEATURES.md; full rounds ~45s-3min), so join friction and reconnect handling matter more than deep session state.
51	- Per-game timers in FEATURES.md: Roast 20s, Confession 15s, Poison Pitch 30s/pitcher, Fill-In 60s, Red Flag 45s, Hot Seat 15s/question, Taboo 60s, Unifying 30s, Alibi 30s, Scatterblast hidden 10-60s.

- Three games carry per-player secret payloads that only own-phones deliver cleanly: Alibi Drop (3 mandatory words to the accused only), Hot Seat Imposter (secret role assignment), Taboo Timer (word+forbidden list to clue-giver and buzzer, hidden from guessers — the Buzzer role is broken on one device).
- Five games are primarily live verbal performance (Poison Pitch, Red Flag Rally, Text Thread Trap, Unifying Theory, Reality Check) — phones should only gate prompts and collect private votes; the comedy must stay spoken.
- Two games get NO benefit or get worse with phones-as-controllers: Scatterblast is explicitly designed around one stationary device with zero handoffs (multi-device timer/audio sync is a liability), and Title Fight is a physical in-room duel.
- Ranking best-to-worst multi-device fit: Fill-In Finisher, Roast Consensus, Over/Under, Confession or Cap, Alibi Drop, Hot Seat Imposter, Taboo Timer, Red Flag Rally, Poison Pitch, Reality Check, Text Thread Trap, Unifying Theory, Title Fight, Scatterblast.
- 10 of 14 games end in a group vote, so a single reusable private-vote + synchronized-reveal component covers most of the redesign surface.
- Reality Check's group score must stay a loud public debate in front of the subject (privatizing it kills the game); only the subject's self-rating is secret.
- Over/Under is uniquely phone-native: the 'receipt' (screen time, unread emails, camera roll count) already lives on the subject's own phone.
- Rounds are short (15-60s timers per FEATURES.md; full rounds ~45s-3min), so join friction and reconnect handling matter more than deep session state.
- Per-game timers in FEATURES.md: Roast 20s, Confession 15s, Poison Pitch 30s/pitcher, Fill-In 60s, Red Flag 45s, Hot Seat 15s/question, Taboo 60s, Unifying 30s, Alibi 30s, Scatterblast hidden 10-60s.

content.content:
1	# research:jackbox-model
2	
3	JACKBOX MODEL RESEARCH — for party-game pivot
4	
5	1) HOW IT WORKS END-TO-END. One person owns/launches the game on a "big screen" host (PC/console/Apple TV/iPad/Fire TV); the game displays a 4-letter room code; everyone else opens jackbox.tv in any browser and enters code + name — no app, no account (https://support.jackboxgames.com/hc/en-us/articles/15794771245975, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Jackbox_Party_Pack). Games run 4–8 (some up to 10) active players; extra joiners become "audience" who vote and influence scoring — up to 10,000 audience since Party Pack 3 (200 in Pack 2) (Wikipedia). Twitch extension lets stream viewers play. Architecture: host game is the authoritative server/game-logic; phones are thin websocket clients. Older packs (≤6) used "Blobcast"/API v1 on socket.io; Pack 7+ uses their "Ecast" API v2 at ecast.jackboxgames.com — room create returns the code, clients identified by participant name; server URL is configurable per minigame via jbg.config.jet. Production Ecast supports multiple concurrent rooms, client reconnection, room passcodes, audiences, and moderation (reverse-engineered private server: https://github.com/InvoxiPlayGames/johnbox). Reconnection UX: a dropped player rejoins by re-entering the same room code + same name; the game holds the seat briefly (https://support.jackboxgames.com/hc/en-us/articles/15794785923223). Browser requirements: websockets, HTML5, JS, cookies.
6	
7	2) PRICING. Party Packs are one-time purchases: recent packs $29.99 (Packs 5–11), older ones $24.99; frequent deep sales (https://www.jackboxgames.com/games/packs, https://steamdb.info/app/331670/). One purchase runs the whole room — friends never pay (Steam forums confirm; core marketing point). Standalones: $9.99 (Quiplash 2 InterLASHional, YDKJ Classic) up to ~$20 (Naughty Pack). Subscription moves: Amazon Luna carries a "Jackbox Games" channel — 50+ games for $4.99/mo (https://luna.amazon.com/subscription/jackbox-games/B09R97C6ZG). Bigger move: a cloud-streamed "Jackbox Games" smart-TV app (AWS GameLift Streams) — free ad-supported starter games, full catalog later via subscription; beta on 1–2 smart-TV platforms (announced Spring-2025 target, blog now says Summer 2026) (https://www.jackboxgames.com/blog/jackbox-coming-to-your-smart-tv-for-free, https://9to5google.com/2025/03/06/jackbox-games-app-is-coming-to-smart-tvs-for-free-in-spring-2025/, https://kotaku.com/jackbox-games-free-smart-tv-fibbage-subscription-1851768236). The Jackbox Megapicker (free, Steam-only, July 2024) is a launcher unifying all owned packs/standalones — fixes "which pack is that game in" friction and cross-sells (https://store.steampowered.com/app/2828500/The_Jackbox_Megapicker/).
8	
9	3) WHY DESIGNERS SAY IT WORKS. CEO Mike Bilder: "the whole phones-as-controllers with multiple players in front of the same TV opened up a lot of doors… everybody's got a phone in their hands, and we want to make the games as accessible as they can be"; and tech must earn its place: "if you could just sit down and open a box of cards… what's really different? … We always want to make sure that there is a reason for the technology to be there" (https://checkpointgaming.net/features/2025/10/chatting-with-mike-bilder-and-allard-laban-about-all-things-jackbox-games/). CCO Allard Laban: "You go to a party and everyone's staring at their phone to begin with… in this way, it was actually bringing people together" (https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/allard-laban-dives-into-the-past-present-and-future-of-jackbox-games). The model emerged with Fibbage (2014) after a failed Ouya partnership; phone controllers + one copy per room became the Party Pack foundation. Stated mission for streaming push: "bring these games to as many people as possible and make them as easy to play as we can." No dedicated Jackbox GDC postmortem surfaced; closest is Harry Gottlieb's 1997 "Jack Principles" talk (https://jackboxgames.fandom.com/wiki/Harry_Gottlieb).
10	
11	4) WEAKNESSES. Needs a shared screen + a host device that runs the game — without a TV the fallback is Zoom/Discord screen-share, where stream lag desyncs the on-screen timer from phone prompts; Jackbox's mitigation is "extended timers" settings, not a real fix (https://support.jackboxgames.com/hc/en-us/articles/15794770038423, https://steamcommunity.com/app/1005300/discussions/0/2244426186201137732/). Packs do ship on iPad/Apple TV/Android TV (host still needed; phones still join via jackbox.tv) but there is no phones-only mode (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/the-jackbox-party-pack/id971879034). Content risk experiment: The Jackbox Naughty Pack (Sept 2024, ~$20, M-rated) got a 68 OpenCritic / 39% recommend — only 3 games, 2 of them reskins; some called it the worst pack to date (https://opencritic.com/game/17275/the-jackbox-naughty-pack). Also: annual $30 pack fatigue and catalog fragmentation (hence Megapicker + standalones like TMP3, Survey Scramble).
12	
13	5) NO-TV JACKBOX-LIKES THAT WORKED. Yes — three proofs: (a) Psych! (Ellen Digital/Warner Bros, 2015, from the Heads Up! team) — every player answers on their own phone, no shared screen; free with $0.99 IAP decks; long-lived hit (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/psych-outwit-your-friends/id1005765746, https://toucharcade.com/2015/10/08/ellen-releases-psych-a-mobile-multiplayer-guessing-game/). (b) Death by AI (Little Umbrella/Playroom, 2023) — browser/Discord/mobile, AI game-master, each player on own device: 20M players in first 3 months, ~7M in weeks, led to $2M raise (a16z speedrun et al.) and profitability (https://gamesbeat.com/little-umbrella-makes-the-funding-rain-after-success-of-death-by-ai-social-game/, https://inworld.ai/case-study/how-inworld-helped-the-ai-game-death-by-ai-with-20-million-players-reach-profitability, https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/30/little-umbrellas-next-jackbox-style-game-pits-you-and-your-friends-against-an-ai-game-show-host/). (c) Gartic Phone (2020) — free browser telephone-drawing game, 2–30 players each on own device, exploded via streamers (https://garticphone.com/). The pattern: drop the TV, keep the room code, monetize free-to-play — the exact lane Death by AI proved with AI-generated content.
14	
15	## KEY FACTS
16	- Jackbox model: one purchase runs the room — host device runs game logic and shows a 4-letter room code; players join at jackbox.tv in any browser with code + name, no app or account; 4-10 active players, overflow becomes voting audience (up to 10,000 since Party Pack 3).
17	- Architecture: phones are thin websocket clients; host is authoritative server; Pack 7+ uses the 'Ecast' API v2 at ecast.jackboxgames.com (older packs used Blobcast/socket.io); production supports reconnection (rejoin with same room code + same name, seat held briefly), room passcodes, and moderation — documented via the johnbox reverse-engineered server (github.com/InvoxiPlayGames/johnbox).
18	- Pricing: Party Packs $29.99 one-time (older $24.99), standalones $9.99-$20; subscription experiments = Amazon Luna channel at $4.99/mo for 50+ games, plus a cloud-streamed free ad-supported smart-TV app (AWS GameLift Streams) with full-catalog subscription planned, beta slipped to Summer 2026.
19	- Why it works per Jackbox CEO Mike Bilder: 'everybody's got a phone in their hands' (zero join friction, max accessibility) and tech must have a purpose beyond a box of cards; CCO Allard Laban: phones at a party 'actually bringing people together.'
20	- Core weakness: the model requires a shared screen plus a host device running the game; remote play falls back to Zoom/Discord screen-share where stream lag desyncs timers — Jackbox's only mitigation is extended-timer settings.
21	- Jackbox has never shipped a phones-only mode; packs run on iPad/Apple TV/Android TV as host devices but players still need the shared screen.
22	- The Jackbox Naughty Pack (Sept 2024, ~$20, M-rated) underperformed: 68 OpenCritic, 39% recommend, 3 games of which 2 were reskins — evidence that raunchy-branding alone doesn't carry a pack.
23	- The Jackbox Megapicker (free, Steam-only, 2024) is a unified launcher fixing catalog fragmentation ('which pack has that game') and cross-selling.
24	- Proof the no-TV lane works: Death by AI (Little Umbrella/Playroom) — browser/Discord/mobile party game with each player on their own phone, AI game master — hit 20M players in 3 months, raised $2M (a16z speedrun), reached profitability.
25	- More no-TV precedents: Psych! (Ellen/Warner Bros, 2015) — own-phone bluffing game, free + $0.99 IAP decks, long-lived hit; Gartic Phone (2020) — free browser drawing-telephone for 2-30 players on their own devices, went viral via streamers.
26	- Strategic pattern for a pivot: keep the room-code join ritual (proven near-zero friction), drop the shared-TV requirement (Jackbox's biggest structural weakness), monetize free-to-play/IAP rather than $30 one-time packs — the exact combination Death by AI validated with AI-generated content.

content.content:
1	# research:phone-party-games
2	
3	PHONE-ONLY PARTY GAME LANDSCAPE, 2025-26
4	
5	1) CATALOG
6	- Psych! (Warner Bros/Ellen, makers of Heads Up!): app-required for all players, room code; same-room focus; freemium (paid decks $0.99-1.99, ad removal ~$10 reported as per-device). Alive but stagnant/decaying — reviews cite crashes every session, 2-min unskippable looping ads, formerly-free decks paywalled (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/psych-outwit-your-friends/id1005765746, https://appgrooves.com/app/psych-outwit-your-friends-by-warner-bros-international-enterprises/negative).
7	- Heads Up! (same studio): the biggest phone-only success — 37M+ downloads, ~$200k/mo iOS revenue still in 2025; one phone as physical prop, paid app on iOS + deck IAP (https://sensortower.com/ios/us/warner-bros/app/heads-up/623592465/, https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.wb.headsup).
8	- Evil Apples (CAH clone): app, free+IAP ($0.99-99.99); remote/async vs strangers; dying — reviews say lobbies rarely fill, users suspect abandonment (https://www.evilapples.com/, https://apps.apple.com/us/app/evil-apples-funny-as/id645705454).
9	- Kahoot: host pays subscription, players join FREE via browser kahoot.it or app with a PIN; free tier now capped at 10 players; alive, revenue mostly corporate/education subs (https://support.kahoot.com/hc/en-us/articles/115003072287, https://triviaeverywhere.com/blog/kahoot-free-plan-guide/).
10	- AirConsole: NOT phone-only (needs TV/PC/car screen; phones=controllers via airconsole.com code). 18M registered users; pivoted hard to automotive (BMW, VW, Audi), acquired by KPIT Oct 15, 2025 (https://grokipedia.com/page/AirConsole, https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2025/06/26/3106037/0/en/AirConsole-and-Audi-Drive-a-New-Era-of-In-Car-Gaming.html).
11	- Death by AI: browser-first (deathbyai.gg link/code), plus iOS/Android apps and Discord Activity; up to 10 players; free + IAP scenario packs; built on Playroom Kit; the breakout AI-native title (https://deathbyai.gg/, https://apps.apple.com/us/app/death-by-ai-party-game/id6474457831).
12	- Plato: app-based "messenger with 50+ games"; remote/strangers focus; 1M+ monthly users, actively updated, appears in MWM's portfolio (https://platoapp.com/en, https://mwm.ai/apps/plato-fun-multiplayer-games/1054747306).
13	- Bunch: rebranded "Bunch is MiniParty!"; last update Jan 2024 — dormant, not officially dead (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/bunch-group-video-chat-games/id1294869021).
14	- Houseparty: dead Oct 2021; Epic shut it after installs fell 83% YoY post-lockdown (https://www.failory.com/cemetery/houseparty, https://variety.com/2021/digital/news/houseparty-shutting-down-1235060236/).
15	- Hint/Hint Master + charades clones: app, forehead-prop genre, freemium; crowded commodity space (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/hint-master-guess-the-word/id1666497757).
16	- PartyDeck: name is fragmented across 3+ unrelated small apps (partydeck.app, partydeck.co, "PartyDeck Game" by Munkyo Seo — "one room, one code, a phone each," all features now free). No dominant player (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/partydeck-game/id6758567392).
17	- Brightful: browser meeting-games for remote work; alive but stagnant (https://www.brightful.me/play/). playingcards.io: alive, free browser rooms w/ code, rooms expire after 2 weeks unless subscribed (https://playingcards.io/docs/faq).
18	- AI-native newcomers: Party AI (MWM), CardYard AI Game Night, GameNight AI, Impromptu (AI-art bluffing), CrowdParty "AI Party Mix" (https://crowdparty.app/game/ai-party-mix, https://impromptu.fun/).
19	- Jackbox itself: launching free ad-supported smart-TV streaming app (beta mid-2025) moving to full-catalog subscription — doubling down on shared screen, still no phone-only product; phones join via browser jackbox.tv (https://kotaku.com/jackbox-games-free-smart-tv-fibbage-subscription-1851768236).
20	
21	2) "ONE PAYS, FRIENDS FREE" ON MOBILE
22	Works only when joiners enter via BROWSER (kahoot.it, jackbox.tv, deathbyai.gg, CrowdParty $18/mo host sub with browser/QR join — https://crowdparty.app/blog/how-to-join-a-crowdparty-game/). Apple guideline: features may only be unlocked via IAP, and entitlements bind to the purchaser's Apple ID — there is no mechanism to grant a friend's device an unlock, hence Psych's hated "$10 per device" ad removal (https://developer.apple.com/documentation/storekit/unlocking-purchased-content). AirConsole Hero is the cleanest in-ecosystem solve: one subscriber per session unlocks all games and kills ads for everyone in the room (https://www.airconsole.com/hero). Session-scoped server-side entitlements (host's account owns the room) are the pattern that passes review.
23	
24	3) NO-SHARED-SCREEN REVEAL UX (5 patterns)
25	(a) Host phone as table/shared screen — Playroom Kit streamMode auto-detects big vs phone screen; host device shows the spectacle, others control (https://docs.joinplayroom.com/features/games/stream). (b) Synchronized reveal on every phone — Psych/Vote's Out/QwiqWit show all answers+votes simultaneously on each device. (c) Phone as physical prop — Heads Up/forehead games; pass-the-phone (Triple Agent, Out of the Loop). (d) Audio/room chaos as the shared channel — Spaceteam. (e) Optional "Big Screen Mode" upgrade when TV exists — Gogogo! (https://www.howtogeek.com/free-party-games-you-can-play-with-just-your-phone/).
26	
27	4) FUN VS LAME VS JACKBOX
28	HN on Death by AI: loved the Jackbox-like social loop and music/animation "perfectly matched to content"; hated AI-judge prompt injection ("I survive" wins), nonsensical verdicts, slow pacing, 100-char limits — fairness of judging is the break point vs Jackbox's peer voting (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=38318889). CBR on Squabble: "players have to provide most of the social elements themselves" — the core criticism of phone-only clones (https://www.cbr.com/jackbox-free-alternative-party-games/). Psych reviews: ad breaks and crashes destroy party pacing. Evil Apples: empty lobbies kill it. Winners share: zero-friction join (browser/code), reveal as a room-wide moment, production polish, and pacing that keeps heads up.
29	
30	## KEY FACTS
31	- The proven 'one person pays, friends join free' mobile pattern is browser-join for players (kahoot.it, jackbox.tv, deathbyai.gg, CrowdParty QR/PIN) with the host holding the paid account, because Apple IAP entitlements bind to the purchaser's Apple ID and cannot unlock friends' devices — Psych's ~$10-per-device ad removal is the cautionary review-bombed counterexample.
32	- AirConsole Hero is the cleanest in-app implementation: one subscriber per session unlocks all games and removes ads for everyone in the room; AirConsole (18M registered users) pivoted to in-car gaming (BMW/VW/Audi) and was acquired by KPIT Oct 15, 2025.
33	- Jackbox itself is moving to a free ad-supported smart-TV streaming app (2025 beta) leading to a full-catalog subscription, and still has no phone-only product — the phone-only, no-TV niche remains structurally open.
34	- Death by AI is the breakout AI-native phone party game: browser-first at deathbyai.gg (also iOS/Android apps and Discord Activities), up to 10 players, free with IAP scenario packs, built on Playroom Kit.
35	- Playroom Kit's streamMode is the reference no-TV UX: it auto-detects large vs phone screens, letting the host phone act as the shared 'table' screen while other phones are controllers (docs.joinplayroom.com/features/games/stream).
36	- Five reveal patterns exist for no-shared-screen play: host phone as table (Playroom), synchronized reveals on all phones (Psych/Vote's Out), phone as physical prop (Heads Up), audio/room chaos (Spaceteam), and optional Big Screen Mode when a TV exists (Gogogo!).
37	- Hacker News verdict on Death by AI: the social loop and audiovisual polish land, but an AI judge that can be prompt-injected ('I do something smart and I survive' always wins) breaks fairness — Jackbox's peer voting avoids this; AI-judged games must handle adversarial inputs.
38	- Psych! (Warner Bros) is alive but decaying: reviews cite crashes every session, 2-minute unskippable looping ads, and formerly-free decks paywalled — ad-interrupted pacing is fatal in a same-room party context.
39	- Heads Up! remains the biggest phone-only party game success (37M+ downloads, ~$200k/month iOS revenue in 2025) via paid app + deck IAP, proving one-phone-as-prop monetizes; Evil Apples is dying of empty lobbies; Houseparty died Oct 2021 after installs fell 83% post-lockdown; Bunch rebranded to 'MiniParty' and has been dormant since Jan 2024.
40	- Kahoot's model: players always free via browser PIN join, host pays subscription, free tier capped at 10 players — host-pays with a hard free player cap is an accepted, familiar mechanic.
41	- The recurring community criticism of phone-only Jackbox clones (CBR on Squabble): 'players have to provide most of the social elements themselves' — the product must manufacture the shared room-wide moment, not just distribute prompts to individual screens.
42	- The name 'PartyDeck' is already fragmented across at least three unrelated small apps (partydeck.app, partydeck.co, PartyDeck Game on iOS) with no dominant owner.

file.content:
# research:phone-party-games

PHONE-ONLY PARTY GAME LANDSCAPE, 2025-26

1) CATALOG
- Psych! (Warner Bros/Ellen, makers of Heads Up!): app-required for all players, room code; same-room focus; freemium (paid decks $0.99-1.99, ad removal ~$10 reported as per-device). Alive but stagnant/decaying — reviews cite crashes every session, 2-min unskippable looping ads, formerly-free decks paywalled (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/psych-outwit-your-friends/id1005765746, https://appgrooves.com/app/psych-outwit-your-friends-by-warner-bros-international-enterprises/negative).
- Heads Up! (same studio): the biggest phone-only success — 37M+ downloads, ~$200k/mo iOS revenue still in 2025; one phone as physical prop, paid app on iOS + deck IAP (https://sensortower.com/ios/us/warner-bros/app/heads-up/623592465/, https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.wb.headsup).
- Evil Apples (CAH clone): app, free+IAP ($0.99-99.99); remote/async vs strangers; dying — reviews say lobbies rarely fill, users suspect abandonment (https://www.evilapples.com/, https://apps.apple.com/us/app/evil-apples-funny-as/id645705454).
- Kahoot: host pays subscription, players join FREE via browser kahoot.it or app with a PIN; free tier now capped at 10 players; alive, revenue mostly corporate/education subs (https://support.kahoot.com/hc/en-us/articles/115003072287, https://triviaeverywhere.com/blog/kahoot-free-plan-guide/).
- AirConsole: NOT phone-only (needs TV/PC/car screen; phones=controllers via airconsole.com code). 18M registered users; pivoted hard to automotive (BMW, VW, Audi), acquired by KPIT Oct 15, 2025 (https://grokipedia.com/page/AirConsole, https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2025/06/26/3106037/0/en/AirConsole-and-Audi-Drive-a-New-Era-of-In-Car-Gaming.html).
- Death by AI: browser-first (deathbyai.gg link/code), plus iOS/Android apps and Discord Activity; up to 10 players; free + IAP scenario packs; built on Playroom Kit; the breakout AI-native title (https://deathbyai.gg/, https://apps.apple.com/us/app/death-by-ai-party-game/id6474457831).
- Plato: app-based "messenger with 50+ games"; remote/strangers focus; 1M+ monthly users, actively updated, appears in MWM's portfolio (https://platoapp.com/en, https://mwm.ai/apps/plato-fun-multiplayer-games/1054747306).
- Bunch: rebranded "Bunch is MiniParty!"; last update Jan 2024 — dormant, not officially dead (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/bunch-group-video-chat-games/id1294869021).
- Houseparty: dead Oct 2021; Epic shut it after installs fell 83% YoY post-lockdown (https://www.failory.com/cemetery/houseparty, https://variety.com/2021/digital/news/houseparty-shutting-down-1235060236/).
- Hint/Hint Master + charades clones: app, forehead-prop genre, freemium; crowded commodity space (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/hint-master-guess-the-word/id1666497757).
- PartyDeck: name is fragmented across 3+ unrelated small apps (partydeck.app, partydeck.co, "PartyDeck Game" by Munkyo Seo — "one room, one code, a phone each," all features now free). No dominant player (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/partydeck-game/id6758567392).
- Brightful: browser meeting-games for remote work; alive but stagnant (https://www.brightful.me/play/). playingcards.io: alive, free browser rooms w/ code, rooms expire after 2 weeks unless subscribed (https://playingcards.io/docs/faq).
- AI-native newcomers: Party AI (MWM), CardYard AI Game Night, GameNight AI, Impromptu (AI-art bluffing), CrowdParty "AI Party Mix" (https://crowdparty.app/game/ai-party-mix, https://impromptu.fun/).
- Jackbox itself: launching free ad-supported smart-TV streaming app (beta mid-2025) moving to full-catalog subscription — doubling down on shared screen, still no phone-only product; phones join via browser jackbox.tv (https://kotaku.com/jackbox-games-free-smart-tv-fibbage-subscription-1851768236).

2) "ONE PAYS, FRIENDS FREE" ON MOBILE
Works only when joiners enter via BROWSER (kahoot.it, jackbox.tv, deathbyai.gg, CrowdParty $18/mo host sub with browser/QR join — https://crowdparty.app/blog/how-to-join-a-crowdparty-game/). Apple guideline: features may only be unlocked via IAP, and entitlements bind to the purchaser's Apple ID — there is no mechanism to grant a friend's device an unlock, hence Psych's hated "$10 per device" ad removal (https://developer.apple.com/documentation/storekit/unlocking-purchased-content). AirConsole Hero is the cleanest in-ecosystem solve: one subscriber per session unlocks all games and kills ads for everyone in the room (https://www.airconsole.com/hero). Session-scoped server-side entitlements (host's account owns the room) are the pattern that passes review.

3) NO-SHARED-SCREEN REVEAL UX (5 patterns)
(a) Host phone as table/shared screen — Playroom Kit streamMode auto-detects big vs phone screen; host device shows the spectacle, others control (https://docs.joinplayroom.com/features/games/stream). (b) Synchronized reveal on every phone — Psych/Vote's Out/QwiqWit show all answers+votes simultaneously on each device. (c) Phone as physical prop — Heads Up/forehead games; pass-the-phone (Triple Agent, Out of the Loop). (d) Audio/room chaos as the shared channel — Spaceteam. (e) Optional "Big Screen Mode" upgrade when TV exists — Gogogo! (https://www.howtogeek.com/free-party-games-you-can-play-with-just-your-phone/).

4) FUN VS LAME VS JACKBOX
HN on Death by AI: loved the Jackbox-like social loop and music/animation "perfectly matched to content"; hated AI-judge prompt injection ("I survive" wins), nonsensical verdicts, slow pacing, 100-char limits — fairness of judging is the break point vs Jackbox's peer voting (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=38318889). CBR on Squabble: "players have to provide most of the social elements themselves" — the core criticism of phone-only clones (https://www.cbr.com/jackbox-free-alternative-party-games/). Psych reviews: ad breaks and crashes destroy party pacing. Evil Apples: empty lobbies kill it. Winners share: zero-friction join (browser/code), reveal as a room-wide moment, production polish, and pacing that keeps heads up.

## KEY FACTS
- The proven 'one person pays, friends join free' mobile pattern is browser-join for players (kahoot.it, jackbox.tv, deathbyai.gg, CrowdParty QR/PIN) with the host holding the paid account, because Apple IAP entitlements bind to the purchaser's Apple ID and cannot unlock friends' devices — Psych's ~$10-per-device ad removal is the cautionary review-bombed counterexample.
- AirConsole Hero is the cleanest in-app implementation: one subscriber per session unlocks all games and removes ads for everyone in the room; AirConsole (18M registered users) pivoted to in-car gaming (BMW/VW/Audi) and was acquired by KPIT Oct 15, 2025.
- Jackbox itself is moving to a free ad-supported smart-TV streaming app (2025 beta) leading to a full-catalog subscription, and still has no phone-only product — the phone-only, no-TV niche remains structurally open.
- Death by AI is the breakout AI-native phone party game: browser-first at deathbyai.gg (also iOS/Android apps and Discord Activities), up to 10 players, free with IAP scenario packs, built on Playroom Kit.
- Playroom Kit's streamMode is the reference no-TV UX: it auto-detects large vs phone screens, letting the host phone act as the shared 'table' screen while other phones are controllers (docs.joinplayroom.com/features/games/stream).
- Five reveal patterns exist for no-shared-screen play: host phone as table (Playroom), synchronized reveals on all phones (Psych/Vote's Out), phone as physical prop (Heads Up), audio/room chaos (Spaceteam), and optional Big Screen Mode when a TV exists (Gogogo!).
- Hacker News verdict on Death by AI: the social loop and audiovisual polish land, but an AI judge that can be prompt-injected ('I do something smart and I survive' always wins) breaks fairness — Jackbox's peer voting avoids this; AI-judged games must handle adversarial inputs.
- Psych! (Warner Bros) is alive but decaying: reviews cite crashes every session, 2-minute unskippable looping ads, and formerly-free decks paywalled — ad-interrupted pacing is fatal in a same-room party context.
- Heads Up! remains the biggest phone-only party game success (37M+ downloads, ~$200k/month iOS revenue in 2025) via paid app + deck IAP, proving one-phone-as-prop monetizes; Evil Apples is dying of empty lobbies; Houseparty died Oct 2021 after installs fell 83% post-lockdown; Bunch rebranded to 'MiniParty' and has been dormant since Jan 2024.
- Kahoot's model: players always free via browser PIN join, host pays subscription, free tier capped at 10 players — host-pays with a hard free player cap is an accepted, familiar mechanic.
- The recurring community criticism of phone-only Jackbox clones (CBR on Squabble): 'players have to provide most of the social elements themselves' — the product must manufacture the shared room-wide moment, not just distribute prompts to individual screens.
- The name 'PartyDeck' is already fragmented across at least three unrelated small apps (partydeck.app, partydeck.co, PartyDeck Game on iOS) with no dominant owner.

content.content:
1	# research:design-theory
2	
3	PARTY GAME DESIGN THEORY — RESEARCH FINDINGS
4	
5	1. DESIGNER WISDOM
6	Jackbox's foundation is Harry Gottlieb's 1997 "Jack Principles" whitepaper (archive.org/details/the-jack-principles; blog.codinghorror.com/the-jack-principles/): maintain pacing (one task at a time, show the program is waiting, move on without slow players), and create/maintain an "illusion of awareness" — the game should feel like a witty host who knows you. Jackbox's modern practice (builtinchicago.org/articles/jackbox-games-design-party-pack; GDC talk gdcvault.com/play/1027202/): never ask multiple choices at once, make inputs efficient, design for the least game-literate person in the room, playtest for laughter not comprehension, and remix proven structures. Their prompt-writing blogs (jackboxgames.com/blog/how-to-write-a-good-quiplash-prompt and /the-science-of-creating-a-quiplash-prompt) teach: seed inherently funny words to license whimsy; if YOU can't think of 3 quick answers, the prompt fails; avoid over-specificity; vary structure; mine real embarrassing memories; ask questions you sincerely want answered. CAH's decay is well documented: players memorize the deck and judges default to "most random card," so play devolves into crudeness (boardgamegeek.com/thread/2797539; cjleo.com/2020/06/22/the-problem-with-cards-against-humanity/ — key flaw: "no real abstraction," you say the joke as yourself). Secret-ballot group voting (Quiplash) beats single-judge (CAH czar): everyone judges every round → less downtime, no judge-taste metagame, and voting itself "fosters fellowship" (medium.com/game-design-fundamentals/a-competitive-analysis-of-quiplash-dcb375fa1069; mechanicsofmagic.com/2024/04/23/critical-play-on-judgement-games-quiplash/). The "machine for moments" thesis: party games are frameworks, not experiences — "know-your-friends" games generate humor from the table, not the cards; ~95% of design effort goes to prompts, not mechanics (loadbearingtomato.com/p/social-game-design-for-parties; cjleo.com/blog/when-winning-doesnt-matter/ — "playtesters never put much stock in who won, and wanted to keep playing"; alexiamandeville.medium.com/game-design-breakdown-party-games-5c2bd301cb96 — "people automatically get 'points' from fellow players for being funny").
7	
8	2. HUMOR THEORY
9	Benign Violation Theory (McGraw/Warren, humorresearchlab.com/benign-violation-theory/): funny = a norm violation perceived as safe, simultaneously. Design levers for "benign": psychological distance, fictional framing, and it-happened-to-someone-else. Targeted humor about people IN the room is the strongest violation available (real stakes) but needs the strongest safety frame. Robert Provine's field research: laughter is ~30x more likely in social settings; only 10-20% of laughter follows actual jokes — laughter is social glue, not joke appreciation (apa.org/monitor/jun06/laughing; pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9489296/). This is why someone else reading your joke aloud lands: anonymity + performance = plausible deniability for the writer, shared attention for the room, and the reader's delivery adds a second comedy layer.
10	
11	3. PACING
12	Jack Principles pacing rules: no dead air, ever; timers force momentum; the game proceeds without stragglers. Simultaneous input (everyone writes at once) crushes sequential turns — the worst phase of CAH is when only the judge can act (Quiplash analysis above). Points should escalate across rounds (final round worth 2-3x) to keep losers in it and build a session climax; scoring is scaffolding, not the point (cjleo.com/blog/when-winning-doesnt-matter/). Avoid elimination — eliminated players leave the magic circle; spotlight rotation ("everyone gets a turn in the spotlight, quiet friends included") beats winner-focus (boardgamedesigncourse.com/game-mechanics-the-elements-of-a-fun-party-game/). Design for organic climaxes: mechanics that exhaust possibilities concentrate stakes naturally (loadbearingtomato.com).
13	
14	4. SOCIAL PSYCHOLOGY
15	Aron's 36-questions study (1997): escalating, reciprocal self-disclosure manufactures closeness in 45 minutes — the mechanism is ESCALATION, each round slightly more personal (ggia.berkeley.edu/practice/36_questions_for_increasing_closeness). Roasting research: prosocial teasing signals trust and regulates group ego, but ONLY when mutual, clearly playful, and built on existing trust (royalsocietypublishing.org/rsbl/article/16/9/20200370/; onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/pere.12567). Safety valves: veto/skip mechanics (Truth-or-Dare's one-veto rule, truthordarezone.com) and opt-out affordances prevent introvert alienation — Pollock found "challenge cannot be refused" was a design flaw, not a feature (loadbearingtomato.com). Calibrate spice as a dial players control, not a fixed level.
16	
17	5. WHY AI CONTENT FALLS FLAT
18	LLM humor research: "ChatGPT is fun, but it is not funny" (arxiv.org/abs/2306.04563) — 90% of 1,008 generated jokes were the same 25 memorized jokes. EMNLP 2025 "Pun Unintended": LLMs pattern-match memorized joke shapes; on unfamiliar puns recognition drops below chance (cardiff.ac.uk/news/view/2981509). 2025 Creativity & Cognition study: LLM humor is formulaic, surface-level phrasing (dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3698061.3734388). Death by AI's HN thread (news.ycombinator.com/item?id=38318889) shows the failure mode in practice: prompt-injectable ("I survive" works), arbitrary judgments, repetitive winning phrases, novelty collapse — its fun came from group dynamics DESPITE the AI. Implication: use AI as prompt-generator/host that sets up HUMANS to be funny about EACH OTHER (personalized, room-specific setups), never as the joke-teller or judge of record. Human-written-quality curation of AI output, personalization to the actual people playing, and group voting as ground truth are the antidotes.
19	
20	## KEY FACTS
21	- Jackbox's Jack Principles (Gottlieb 1997): one task at a time, zero dead air, game proceeds without slow players, and an 'illusion of awareness' that makes the game feel like a witty host (archive.org/details/the-jack-principles).
22	- Jackbox prompt-writing rules: seed inherently funny words, reject any prompt you can't answer 3 ways fast, avoid over-specificity, mine real embarrassing memories (jackboxgames.com/blog/how-to-write-a-good-quiplash-prompt).
23	- CAH decays because players memorize the finite deck and judges default to 'most random card'; its single-judge phase idles everyone else — secret-ballot group voting (Quiplash) fixes both and 'fosters fellowship' (medium.com/game-design-fundamentals/a-competitive-analysis-of-quiplash-dcb375fa1069).
24	- Party games are 'machines for generating moments': ~95% of design effort belongs in prompts/content, not mechanics; points are scaffolding — playtesters ignore who won and just want to keep playing (loadbearingtomato.com/p/social-game-design-for-parties; cjleo.com/blog/when-winning-doesnt-matter/).
25	- Benign Violation Theory: funny = norm violation + perceived safety, simultaneously; targeted humor about people in the room is the strongest material but requires the strongest safety framing (humorresearchlab.com/benign-violation-theory/).
26	- Provine: laughter is ~30x more likely socially and only 10-20% follows actual jokes — design for shared attention and performance (someone else reading your anonymous joke aloud), not joke quality (apa.org/monitor/jun06/laughing).
27	- Aron's 36-questions effect: escalating reciprocal self-disclosure manufactures closeness in ~45 minutes — sessions should ramp personal-ness round by round (ggia.berkeley.edu/practice/36_questions_for_increasing_closeness).
28	- Roasting bonds friends only when mutual, clearly playful, and trust-based; include a veto/skip safety valve and player-controlled spice dial — forced participation is a documented design flaw (royalsocietypublishing.org/rsbl/article/16/9/20200370/).
29	- Prefer simultaneous input, spotlight rotation, escalating round multipliers, and no elimination; dead air and single-actor phases are the top pacing killers.
30	- LLMs are provably unfunny as joke-tellers: 90% of ChatGPT's jokes were 25 memorized templates (arxiv.org/abs/2306.04563), and pun recognition drops below chance on novel material (EMNLP 2025).
31	- Death by AI's HN postmortem: prompt-injectable, arbitrary, repetitive AI judging killed novelty fast; fun survived only via group dynamics — so use AI to set up humans to be funny about each other, never as the joke-teller or final judge (news.ycombinator.com/item?id=38318889).
